category: AML - structuring / smurfing to evade cash-transaction reporting thresholds
risk_signals: Multiple cash deposits kept just below the mandatory reporting threshold
Deposits split across several branches, accounts, or consecutive days by one or related parties
Cash activity inconsistent with the customer's stated profile or business
analyst_reasoning: Structuring breaks a large sum into many sub-threshold transactions specifically to stay under Cash Transaction Report limits. No single deposit looks reportable, so the fraud only reveals itself in the aggregate pattern across accounts, branches, and time.
investigation_steps: Aggregate deposits by customer and connected parties across accounts, branches, and dates
Test proximity of amounts to the reporting threshold and the regularity of splitting
Compare cash volumes against the declared income or business turnover
decision: File a Suspicious Transaction Report, place the customer under enhanced monitoring, and review linked accounts for a smurfing network.
outcome: Structured funds enter the system layered and hard to trace; detection depends on aggregation logic rather than single-transaction rules.
lessons_learned: Sub-threshold deposits are only visible as fraud in aggregate, so monitoring must aggregate across accounts and time
Amounts clustering just under the reporting limit are a hallmark of structuring
Cash inconsistent with customer profile is the anchoring red flag
